title: How to Govern Autonomous Agents in Enterprise AI Factories | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: Nvidia publishes a promotional technical reference architecture ('Secure Agent Workspace') for governing autonomous AI agents at enterprise scale, splitting a presentation layer (user device) from a managed execution layer (per-user VM), and prescribing SSO, default-deny networking, human approval gates, sandboxing, and centralized audit logging — using its own internal 40,000-employee deployment as a proof point.

insights.claim: Nvidia's Secure Agent Workspace Reference Design's central architectural move is separating the presentation layer (the user's laptop, browser, IDE, or terminal) from the execution layer (a managed workspace), so that identity, network access, credentials, runtime policy, audit, and human review can all be enforced consistently outside the user's device.
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: A reusable enterprise security pattern: as agentic AI moves from chat to autonomous multi-hour task execution with access to sensitive systems, the local endpoint stops being trusted to run agent logic — relevant for any enterprise security/IT team designing agent governance ahead of broad agent rollouts.
insights.quote: the user's laptop, browser, integrated development environment (IDE), or terminal serves as the presentation layer, not the execution layer. Agent execution occurs in a managed workspace where identity, network access, credentials, runtime policy, audit, and human review can be enforced consistently.
insights.locator: The NVIDIA Secure Agent Workspace Reference Design introduces a clear architectural shift

insights.claim: Nvidia frames AI agents as having moved 'beyond chat' to inspecting code, running tests, reading documents, querying internal systems, and operating for hours on a user's behalf — implying agent capability has outpaced existing enterprise security models built around chatbots.
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: Signals the security/governance tooling market opportunity created by agent capability growth — a category Nvidia is positioning itself to serve alongside compute sales, extending its enterprise AI stack beyond hardware/software into governance tooling.
insights.quote: AI agents are quickly moving beyond chat. They inspect code, run tests, read documents, search knowledge bases, query internal systems, and operate for hours on behalf of a user.
insights.locator: AI agents are quickly moving beyond chat.

insights.claim: Phase I of the reference design secures the perimeter around the workspace: a dedicated company-managed VM per user, SSO-gated login, default-deny network access with only pre-approved connections allowed, mandatory human approval for system-changing agent actions, and centralized logging of all workspace activity.
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: A concrete checklist enterprises can benchmark their own agent deployments against; the default-deny network posture is the key control that limits blast radius from a compromised or misbehaving agent.
insights.quote: Block all internet traffic by default. Only allow connections to specific, pre-approved internal and external services.
insights.locator: Secure the perimeter outside the virtual machine

insights.claim: Phase II adds runtime security inside the VM: active sandboxing via a dedicated runtime (Nvidia OpenShell) that watches every action in real time, centrally signed security policy bundles, credential protection via a secrets proxy so the agent never sees raw secrets, and continuous verification that security rules are active before every agent action.
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: Introduces 'NVIDIA OpenShell' as a named product/component for agent sandboxing — a concrete technology to track as Nvidia builds out an agent-governance product line adjacent to its GPU/software stack.
insights.quote: Run the agent inside a dedicated runtime (such as NVIDIA OpenShell) that watches every action in real-time... Don't store passwords or secret keys directly in the workspace. Use a secure proxy that handles those keys behind the scenes so the agent never sees the raw secrets.
insights.locator: Add Runtime Security Inside the Virtual Machine

insights.claim: Nvidia recommends 'blast radius' governance controls that require human review before specific high-impact actions (e.g., merging code, changing ticket status), with all logs piped out in Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework (OCSF) format for audit readiness.
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: Names a specific audit-log standard (OCSF) enterprises should adopt for agent governance — relevant for compliance teams building interoperable logging pipelines across security tools.
insights.quote: Set up "blast radius" controls. Define which actions (like merging code or changing ticket status) require human review before execution, and make sure all logs are piped out in Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework (OCSF) format so they're ready for audit.

insights.claim: Nvidia's reference design offers two concrete deployment paths: Red Hat OpenShift Virtualization for on-premises (using NetworkPolicy, EgressFirewall, and GitOps-managed VM profiles) or Microsoft Azure for cloud-native (using Azure Firewall Premium, Workload Identity Federation, Azure Key Vault over Private Endpoints, and Azure Compute Gallery for golden images).
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: Explicitly names Red Hat/OpenShift and Microsoft Azure as the two supported deployment substrates for Nvidia's enterprise agent governance stack — signals a continued deep partnership between Nvidia and Microsoft/Red Hat in enterprise AI infrastructure rather than a self-contained Nvidia cloud offering.
insights.quote: Setting up the workspace starts with choosing Red Hat OpenShift Virtualization for on-premises environments, or Microsoft Azure for cloud-native deployments.
insights.locator: Deploy the secure agent workspace on-prem or in the cloud

insights.claim: Nvidia recommends GitOps as the mechanism for managing policy intent (VM profiles, network rules, policy metadata, release information stored in Git), with signed runtime policy bundles distributed through a controlled release channel, decoupling declared policy state from runtime enforcement.
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: A specific operational pattern (GitOps + signed policy bundles) that treats agent governance like infrastructure-as-code — relevant for DevSecOps teams evaluating how to make agent policy changes auditable and repeatable rather than ad hoc.
insights.quote: Store VM profiles, network rules, policy metadata, and release information in Git. GitOps should reconcile the desired platform state, while signed runtime policy bundles are distributed through a controlled release channel.

insights.claim: Nvidia's own IT-managed internal AI factory (led by distinguished solutions architect Nic Borensztein) serves inference and agentic applications to 40,000 employees, presented as a proof point that this governance architecture operates at real enterprise scale.
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: A concrete scale data point (40,000 internal users) that investors/enterprises can use to gauge how far along Nvidia is in dogfooding its own agent governance stack before selling it externally — a credibility signal, though self-reported.
insights.quote: He is the lead architect for the NVIDIA IT-managed AI factory, serving inference and agentic apps to 40,000 employees internally.

insights.claim: Nvidia proposes 'blueprints' as repeatable workflow templates configured with goal, required tools, allowed services, data scope, write permissions, review gates, and logging expectations, from which agent developers make minimal modifications rather than building governance from scratch each time.
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: Templated governance blueprints could lower the marginal cost of deploying new compliant agents across an enterprise, potentially accelerating internal agent proliferation once the initial governance investment is made.
insights.quote: Each blueprint is configured with its goal, required tools, allowed services, data scope, write permissions, review gates, and logging expectations.
insights.locator: Set up agent blueprints for the agent workspace

insights.claim: Agent identity in Nvidia's model is tied back to a human user or sponsor through SSO via a 'delegation record,' and secrets are never hardcoded but instead accessed via short-lived capability tokens issued through a credential proxy.
insights.speaker: Alex Sandu
insights.so_what: Establishes a clear accountability chain (agent-to-human-sponsor mapping) directly relevant to the accountability/blame-diffusion concerns raised elsewhere in AI-agent governance debates — a concrete technical control against the 'who's responsible for the agent's actions' problem.
insights.quote: Register the agent with a logical identity that ties back to the user or sponsor through SSO. Use a delegation record to define exactly what the agent is allowed to do.
